If there’s one thing that can really mess up a freelancer’s day, it’s a brownout. No electricity, no internet, and missed deadlines—it’s the perfect recipe for stress. Living in the Philippines, where brownouts are a fact of life, especially during typhoons, you either adapt or risk falling behind.

Let me share something that’s saved me over and over again. Actually, three things: the Ingco 900W portable generator, the V380 PRO Mini UPS, and the APC Easy UPS BVX 650VA. These aren’t just random gadgets; they’ve become essentials in my freelancing setup and have even helped during emergencies.

Oh, and before we dive into the details, let me just say—I’m not getting paid to talk about these products. There’s no sponsorship or commission involved here. I’m writing this because I’ve used these tools, I know they work, and they’ve made my life so much easier. If you’re looking for ways to power through a brownout without breaking the bank, I can confidently say this setup is worth considering.

The Ingco 900W Generator: My Power Buddy

I didn’t think I’d need a generator until I got tired of being at the mercy of brownouts. The Ingco 900W portable generator caught my attention because it’s portable, easy to use, and affordable (I got mine during a Christmas sale for just a little over ₱6,000—talk about a steal!). Normally, it’s priced at ₱13,570, but even at full price, it’s a solid investment for backup power.

Here’s how I use it: I can power my laptop, an electric fan, and charge my phone and tablet all at once. For those wondering, no, it can’t handle heavy appliances like a rice cooker or fridge—it’s only 900 watts. But for keeping your home office running, it’s perfect.

Operating it is straightforward. The generator has a recoil start, a choke lever, a power switch, and a single AC outlet. Starting it takes just a few steps, and shutting it down is equally simple. When refueling, it’s important to use a voltage regulator or extension cord to avoid damaging your devices. At minimal load, a full tank of gas lasts about six hours, but I recommend checking and planning to refuel every four hours to be safe.

Now, let’s talk about quirks. The noise? Loud. It’s definitely an outdoor-only tool. The exhaust smell is another thing that will remind you why ventilation matters. You’ll also need to mix gasoline with 2T oil, so keep that in mind before you start it up.

Despite the noise and other quirks, this generator has been my go-to for long brownouts. Whether you’re working on a big project, taking care of kids who can’t sleep in the heat, or just trying to keep things running smoothly during a storm, the Ingco 900W is a great starting point for backup power.

The V380 PRO Mini UPS: Keeping Me Connected

Electricity is one thing, but no internet? That’s another level of panic. After some research (and a bit of skepticism), I found the V380 PRO Mini UPS, which I bought from Shopee for Php 303.00 (plus shipping). It’s a small device that acts as a battery for your modem or router during outages. To be honest, I thought it might be a scam, but curiosity won—and I’m glad it did.

Now, I have three of these little lifesavers. They’re always plugged in, charging their batteries and ready to take over the moment the power cuts out. The best part? The intelligent charging system stops once the battery is full and simply routes power directly to your modem. No worries about overheating or fire hazards.

You might be wondering why I use a Modem UPS when I already have a generator. The answer is simple: convenience and speed. With the UPS, I don’t have to scramble to unplug the modem and plug it into the generator’s extension cord during a brownout. Plus, the electricity saved by powering the modem with the UPS can be allocated to something else on the generator. It’s a win-win for both ease of use and efficiency.

I have the 5400mAh version, and while I haven’t officially timed it, calculations show it can keep a standard modem running for around 4.3 hours. That’s plenty of time to finish tasks, send emails, or wrap up a Zoom call.

It’s also replaceable, so when the battery starts to lose capacity, you can upgrade to a bigger one. Lightweight and compact, it’s perfect not just for freelancers but also for families needing internet access during emergencies.

The APC Easy UPS BVX 650VA: A Lifesaver for My Laptop and Monitor

Now, let’s talk about the latest addition to my arsenal: the APC Easy UPS BVX 650VA, which I bought for ₱2,548 at PC Logic Cabanatuan. This entry-level UPS protects my laptop and monitor during sudden outages. It does more than just power my devices; it gives me enough time to save my work and start the generator without panicking.

The UPS has four outlets that provide battery backup and surge protection. It also has an Automatic Voltage Regulation (AVR) feature, which stabilizes voltage fluctuations. That’s a fancy way of saying it prevents power spikes from damaging or frying your equipment.

Another thing I love is that it’s compatible with generators. When I switch to the Ingco generator, the UPS keeps everything stable, ensuring a smooth transition without damaging my laptop.

For those with gaming setups, this is a must-have. I use another UPS specifically for my TV and PS5, giving me enough time to turn off the console properly during an outage. This prevents data corruption or damage—a relief for anyone who knows how heartbreaking a botched shutdown can be.

Why This Setup Matters

This blog was inspired by two back-to-back unscheduled power outages. At 5 a.m., a sudden brownout woke me up (thanks, Celcor Cabanatuan). By 9 a.m., the whole city was out of power again (thanks, NGCP). Scrolling through Facebook, I saw freelancers panicking and asking for the best power backup options.

This setup isn’t just for freelancers like me. Parents with babies who can’t sleep in the humidity, or anyone who needs reliable power during typhoons, can benefit from these tools.

Brownouts are frustrating, but they don’t have to ruin your day. With the Ingco generator, V380 PRO Mini UPS, and APC Easy UPS, you can stay productive, connected, and even comfortable.

What’s your go-to setup for surviving brownouts? I’d love to hear what works for you—or if you’ve got questions about these tools, let’s chat!

There’s a strange feeling I get when I see public officials doing something that looks too perfect. Not evil. Not even suspicious. Just... off. Like a government photo where everyone's smiling, the timing is convenient, and the message feels a little too polished.

At first, akala mo genuine. Pero habang tinitingnan mo, may bumubulong na tanong—Sino ba talaga ang pinaglilingkuran dito? Because sometimes, it doesn’t feel like it’s for us. It feels like it’s for the camera.

The election season is over. The rallies have died down, the slogans have faded, and the motorcade routes are back to being regular traffic jams. But the performances haven’t stopped. We’re no longer watching campaigns—we’re watching governance dressed up to look like charity, care, and control.

And the word for that? Optics.

## What Is Political Optics?

We hear the word thrown around every now and then—optics. Usually during scandals. Or when a government official does something suspiciously camera-friendly.

In politics, optics is the art of managing public perception. It’s not about the real impact of a decision or action—it’s about how it looks to the public. It’s not about what you did. It’s about how it played.

Cambridge defines it as “the public's opinion and understanding of a situation after seeing it as the media presents it.” Merriam-Webster goes a step further, calling it the part of any decision “that relates to public perception.”

In short: kung mukha kang tumutulong, kahit wala ka namang talagang nagawang makabuluhan para sa mga tao, pwede na.

The term originally comes from science—optics, as in light and lenses. But over time, it evolved. From cameras to spin doctors, the meaning shifted. It used to be about light. Now it’s about spotlight.

And let’s be fair: optics on its own isn’t evil. Sometimes, it helps simplify a message. It brings attention to things that matter. But the problem starts when the image becomes the only thing that matters. Pero iba na ang usapan kapag yung itsura na lang ang mahalaga—kahit walang laman yung ginagawa.

And that’s where the danger begins.

We saw this play out when Vice President Sara Duterte, in the middle of mounting investigations and impeachment threats, stood at a press conference and said, “They can drag me to hell. Pagdating nila doon, ako pa rin ang presidente ng impyerno.” It was fiery, intense, and made headlines. But it was also optics. A performance meant to project toughness, unshakable resolve, and martyrdom in one go.

On the other side, President Bongbong Marcos responded to Duterte’s more explosive threat days later—not with anger, but silence. He waited three days before releasing a video. It was deliberate. Calculated restraint designed to make him look like the rational, presidential figure in the room.

These are optics in action. Two very different styles—both intentional. And both telling us exactly who they want to be seen as.

When politicians rely on optics to cover up failures…
When they use photo ops instead of solutions…
When it becomes less about the people and more about controlling the spotlight…
You’re not solving anything. You’re just putting on a good show.

And sadly, in the Philippines, we’ve been applauding the show for so long, minsan nakakalimutan na natin na may kailangan talagang ayusin.

## How Optics Works (and Why It’s So Effective in the Philippines)

Optics works because it doesn't ask you to think. It makes you feel. That’s where its power lies—in emotion. Before we even get the full story, our reactions have already been shaped by images, tone, and framing. Bago pa man tayo magtanong ng “ano ba talaga ang nangyari?”—naka-react na tayo. That’s how politicians win—by beating your brain to the punch.

They don’t need to convince you with data. They just need a moment that makes you believe something is true. Something comforting. Or something enraging. Either way, they win your attention.

## The Playbook: Familiar Tactics That Still Work

Most optics strategies follow the same formula. And yet, kahit obvious na minsan, effective pa rin.

There’s the ceremonial photo op—the shovel, the ribbon cutting, the jacket over a barong in a flood zone.
The dramatic quote, carefully delivered, made for headlines.
The well-timed announcement right after a scandal breaks, hoping to bury bad news beneath noise.
And then, there’s the safe space press conference—usually with a friendly vlogger or a controlled setting. Tough questions? Hindi kasama sa script.

Even old slogans are recycled to trigger nostalgia or pretend continuity. Bagong something. Panibagong ano. Branding is the substitute for progress.

Sometimes, the show becomes so stylized, it detaches from the real world. During Typhoon Kristine in October 2024, Camarines Sur officials—Rep. Miguel Luis Villafuerte and Gov. Luigi Villafuerte—were criticized for their relief distribution photos that looked more like movie posters than disaster response.

In contrast, Leni Robredo—also responding to Typhoon Kristine—was photographed wading through floodwaters in Naga, distributing food. It went viral too. The difference? Her presence was consistent with her past actions, but even then, critics pointed to it as optics. This shows that even authentic actions can be framed through the lens of perception.

And then there’s the other kind of optic—the one that shows absence instead of action. In July 2024, while Super Typhoon Carina ravaged parts of Luzon, Sara Duterte left the country for a personal trip to Germany to attend a concert. Her office confirmed the trip, but the timing was a massive optics disaster. It projected a lack of urgency and empathy, which she later tried to fix with aggressive press conferences.

### 1. Culture of Performance
We’ve blurred the lines between politics and showbiz. Kung sino ang kilala, siya ang bida. Kung sino ang malakas ang dating, siya ang inaalala. Sometimes, even the most outrageous behavior becomes acceptable if it makes for good TV.

### 2. Weak Institutions
In a system where political parties are interchangeable, and ideologies barely matter, image becomes the platform. Wala nang issue-based debate. Wala ring consistency sa paninindigan. Ang meron? Branding. Messaging. Optics.

### 3. Transactional Politics
Many Filipinos still vote based on who gave ayuda, sinong tumulong sa nasunugan, or who sponsored the town fiesta. And so, the visible act of helping, kahit pansamantala lang, becomes the currency of politics.

### 4. Media and Misinformation Ecosystem
It’s too easy now to manufacture perception. There are troll armies. Influencer propaganda. News articles that read like campaign material. Even state media gets used for damage control instead of truth. And once the algorithm picks up the image, it spreads faster than any fact-check can catch up.

## From Campaigns to Governance

What’s dangerous about optics is that it doesn’t stop when the campaign ends. It just changes costume. Now, even in governance, officials use the same strategies:
- Redirect blame
- Manage headlines
- Stage goodwill
- Soften scandal with drama

Performance becomes policy. Perception becomes progress. But the problems? Still there. Optics works because we let it. Because we’re wired to react, and the system was built to exploit that.

## How to Spot Optics in Philippine Politics

Sometimes, it’s loud. Other times, it hides behind a smile, a slogan, or a carefully chosen background. But the more you notice, the harder it becomes to ignore. Optics isn’t complicated. It thrives on predictability.

### 1. Gimmicks That Generate Applause but Solve Nothing
Some events are designed to impress at first glance—grand crowds, bright lights, statements delivered from behind a thick mahogany desk. I saw this up close at the Bagong Pilipinas rally in January 2024. I was backstage at Quirino Grandstand, sitting quietly beside top government officials while the crowd roared outside. But what struck me was this: the moment I arrived, all I heard was talk of attendance. People were checking their watches, counting rows, and coordinating transportation for supporters who were bused in. It turns out attendance was mandatory. The longer I stayed, the clearer it became. There were no major policy directions. No real reform announced. It was just a rally to show that the administration still had numbers. All pomp, no substance.

### 2. Suspicious Timing
Timing is one of optics’ sharpest tools. It often shows up not when it’s needed most—but when a politician needs a distraction. That’s exactly what happened in May 2025, after the administration’s poor performance in the midterm elections. President Marcos didn’t address the failures. Instead, he announced a massive cabinet reshuffle. On paper, it sounded like bold leadership. But in context, it was textbook optics. A gesture designed to say “I am taking charge,” without actually changing the systemic problems that caused the loss.

### 3. Selective Access, Safe Questions
Real leadership faces tough questions. Optics prefers friendly rooms. When you notice politicians granting interviews only to vloggers, or doing live streams with influencers instead of veteran journalists, you’re watching optics. It’s the media version of singing to your own choir. The message goes out, but it never gets questioned.

### 4. Slogans and Symbols Without Direction
You’ll know it’s optics when the energy goes into branding instead of systems. Logos. Hashtags. New color schemes. Department-wide rebrands. All under the name of transformation—but the front desk is still slow, and the services are still lacking. Kung maraming kulay pero walang nilalaman, optics ‘yan.

### 5. Public Acts Without Public Plans
Here’s another test: after the ribbon-cutting or press conference, what comes next? Who’s in charge of implementation? Where’s the budget? What’s the timeline? Who’s being held accountable? Programs without follow-through aren’t solutions. They’re campaigns in disguise.

### 6. The Gut Check: Ask the Right Questions
You don’t need credentials to spot optics. Start with the basics:
- Is this solving something real?
- Would this still happen if there were no cameras?
- Is there a plan, or just a photo?
- Who actually benefits here?

Go beyond the headlines. Ask who funded the initiative. Ask where the data is. And remember: kung hindi malinaw ang sagot, malamang hindi serbisyo ang pinapanood mo.

## What to Do When You Spot Optics

So now you’ve seen it. You know what optics looks like. The question is—what do you do?

### 1. Don’t React Right Away
Optics thrives on impulse. It’s built to stir emotion—admiration, outrage, pity—so you’ll immediately react. We saw this clearly during the 2024 budget hearing when Sara Duterte was questioned about ₱73 million in COA disallowances. Instead of explaining the expenses, she asked, “Bakit mo ako kinu-crucify?” and refused to entertain further questions. Religious imagery, personal attacks, and victimhood framing. This wasn’t a meltdown. It was a trigger—meant to provoke defenders and distract critics. So the next time you see a leader go viral for being “fierce” or “emotional” under pressure, pause. Ask: What didn’t get answered? What did they want me to feel instead of know?

### 2. Trace the Source and the Timing
Optics is rarely spontaneous. It’s timed to serve a need. In September 2024, Sara Duterte skipped the second budget hearing entirely. By skipping the hearings and playing with technicalities, she avoided hard questions—then reframed the entire thing as a “political attack” by the House. That’s how optics works: it hides in the timing.

### 3. Talk About It—But Smartly
You don’t need to go on rants. Just talk to people around you. Point out patterns. For example: “Napansin mo ba? Tuwing may issue sa budget, palaging may presscon. Pero wala namang paliwanag.” Remember her “drag me to hell” press conference in October 2024? That was the day after President Marcos said he felt “deceived” by her. In response, she held a two-hour press event—shouting, posturing, and throwing all the blame back. But notice what she didn’t do: answer where the funds went.

### 4. Shift the Conversation to the Real Work
When someone praises a politician’s bold words or dramatic moves, steer the conversation toward outcomes. Ask: “Okay, pero may na-liquidate na ba?” “Ano bang ginagawa niya to fix it?” When Duterte made death threats against the President, First Lady, and Speaker of the House in November, it dominated headlines. The whole episode drowned out what the House was actually investigating: ₱612 million in confidential funds.

### 5. Support Transparency Where It Happens
It’s easy to feel like everyone’s just performing. But some still show up and answer tough questions. Point out the difference between leadership and theater. Sara Duterte’s long string of press conferences—from her “bloodbath” line in June 2025 to her claims of crucifixion—none of them answered the questions the public deserved to hear.

### 6. Stay Skeptical, But Not Hopeless
Discernment isn’t cynicism. It’s protection. It’s how we learn to look beyond the drama and into the delivery. Hindi mo kailangang maging eksperto. Pero kailangan mong tumigil at magtanong. You don’t need perfect leaders. You just need leaders who won’t hide behind theatrics when things get hard.

## Conclusion: Seeing Through the Smoke and Selfies

I wasn’t always this observant. There was a time I’d see a politician crying on camera and think, “Uy, at least may ginagawa.” That was before I realized that doing something isn’t the same as performing something.

The more I learned about optics, the more I noticed the patterns. What used to impress me now feels rehearsed. What used to anger me now makes me stop and ask, “What are they distracting me from?”

Hindi ko sinasabing masama ang optics. Minsan kailangan talaga. Public service still needs public trust. But trust should be earned through work, not manufactured through a lens. That’s when optics stops being harmless and starts becoming dangerous.

So here’s what I learned: you don’t have to be loud to resist it. You just have to notice. Keep asking questions. Look for the silence behind the speech. Don’t clap just because it looks good. If it looks too perfect, ask yourself if it actually worked. And if it didn’t? Then you’ve spotted the optics. And that’s your cue to stop watching—and start paying attention.

In a market flooded with AI-generated content, clients are rediscovering the unique value of skilled freelance writers who bring a human touch. This blog explores the shift back to authentic, experience-driven writing, why SEO, AEO, and EEAT matter more than ever, and how building credibility through real, verifiable work sets true professionals apart.

You’ve probably seen it too—those articles online that just don’t feel right. They’re polished, sure, but there’s something missing. It’s almost like the writing has no soul. That’s what happens when AI takes over the content creation process. And I can tell you, clients are noticing. More and more, they’re coming back to people like us—writers who can do more than just string words together.

I remember when ChatGPT 3.5 first launched. A bunch of my clients took a break, eager to see if the tools could replace what we’d been working on. I wasn't offended—I was curious myself. But after a few months, they trial-and-errored back with the same thing: “The words were fine, but it felt empty.” That was a lightbulb moment for me. AI can be a great tool, but it can’t replace the experience of someone who’s been writing and connecting with readers for years.

So, let’s dive into why clients are turning back to real writers and what’s behind this shift.

KEY POINTS:
• The surge of AI-generated content has led clients back to human writers who offer depth, authenticity, and a personal touch.
• Skilled freelance writers who blend traditional writing with SEO, AEO, and EEAT expertise are in high demand.
• The importance of cultural nuances: AI content often misses local context and tone, making human writers indispensable for relatability.
• Personal anecdotes show the shift in client needs, from testing AI tools to returning for genuine, experience-driven writing.
• Increased client invitations have prompted considerations for expanding into a team or agency setup as the trend continues.
• Establishing credibility through a public blog helps bridge the gap when NDAs prevent sharing ghostwritten work, showcasing ongoing expertise.

The Evolution of My Writing Journey
When I started writing, it was all about the story. There were no algorithms, no keyword stuffing—just a writer and their thoughts. My goal was always to make sure the reader felt something. I had this one client early on who wanted a series of blogs that felt like a conversation between friends. No SEO tricks, no ranking goals, just genuine engagement. It was simpler back then, but the landscape was already changing.

Then SEO came into the picture. I quickly realized it wasn’t enough to write well; I needed to learn how to weave in keywords without making it sound forced. It felt awkward at first, like trying to speak a new language. But I saw the potential of SEO—it wasn’t just about getting clicks; it was about reaching the right people with the right content.

Why Clients Are Coming Back to Human Writers
In the last couple of years, AI content has flooded the market. It’s fast and efficient, but there’s a catch. It often lacks the cultural nuances and local tone that make content relatable. I had a British client who hired a writer through a freelancing site. The writer was using AI, and the tone was all wrong, filled with American expressions that just didn’t fit the UK audience. “This doesn’t sound like us,” the client said, frustrated. That’s when it hit me: AI might be great at generating text, but it misses the mark when it comes to the little details that make writing feel authentic.

Lately, I’ve been getting more invites than ever, not just from Upwork but also from clients on onlinejobs.ph. They’re explicitly looking for writers who can balance using AI tools with adding a human touch. If this trend continues, I might have to consider hiring a team of writers to help manage the workload. Who knows—this could even turn into an agency one day. But for now, I’m managing fine on my own, and it’s been exciting to see this increased demand.

Breaking Down SEO, AEO, and EEAT
Let me tell you what makes a skilled writer stand out these days: it’s a mix of traditional writing, SEO, AEO, and EEAT.

• SEO (Search Engine Optimization):
Back when I first heard about SEO, I thought it was just a fancy way to game the system. But I quickly learned it’s a key part of getting content noticed. My first attempts felt clunky, like trying to fit square pegs into round holes. But I kept at it and eventually figured out how to make it work without sacrificing the natural flow of the story.

• AEO (Algorithm Experience Optimization):
AEO focuses on making content work for answer engines like Google’s featured snippets and voice searches. This is different from traditional SEO, which aims to rank high in search results. One of my clients was frustrated because their content wasn't showing up in voice searches. We realized it wasn’t structured to answer common questions directly. So, I reworked it—added schema markup, optimized for natural language, and made sure it addressed the most frequent queries. That project was an eye-opener for me, showing how important it is to cater to the way people actually search for information now, especially with the rise of voice search.

• EEAT (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, and Trustworthiness):
A lot of my clients have told me they chose me because they trusted my experience. They didn’t want generic, AI-generated info; they wanted someone who had lived it and could offer real insights. That’s where the “Experience” part of EEAT comes in. It’s about more than just knowing your stuff—it’s about showing you’ve been there. Combine that with Expertise, Authoritativeness, and Trustworthiness, and you get content that not only ranks well but actually resonates with readers.

Establishing Credibility Through My Blog
One thing that’s worked well for me is maintaining an actual blog that I update weekly. This isn’t just a portfolio; it’s a live demonstration of my writing style and expertise. It shows potential clients that I’m active and that I write and publish consistently. The reality is, about 90% of my contracts are ghostwriting gigs covered by non-disclosure agreements (NDAs). Even if a client asks for samples of my work, I can’t share most of it because of those legal barriers. It’s a tough spot because I have to choose between integrity and transparency. That’s why I maintain a public blog. It allows me to point to something and say, “Here is what I can do.” It’s a way for clients to see my style and verify my skills for themselves. In fact, this very article is part of that strategy—it’s meant to give you an actual glimpse into how I write and what I offer.

Why Reputation Matters More Than Ever
If this blog has you thinking about diving into freelance writing, pause for a second. It’s not just about stringing words together—it’s about building a reputation that clients can rely on. I’ve had clients say, “We picked you because we could verify your work, not just look at samples.” They’re digging deeper these days, reaching out to previous clients and checking feedback. This isn’t just a challenge for new writers; even those of us with years of experience have to prove ourselves repeatedly.

An old client came back to me recently after trying out cheaper writers who relied heavily on AI. They were honest: “We realized it wasn’t about just having content; it was about having content that felt genuine.” I’ve heard similar stories lately, and it’s a clear sign clients value quality and authenticity more than ever before.

Wrapping It Up
The content market might be overflowing with AI-generated articles, but I’m seeing a shift. Clients are rediscovering the value of the human touch—the kind of connection and understanding that AI can’t fill. They’re coming back to writers who can offer depth, connection, and a real understanding of the nuances that make content relatable. It’s not just about cranking out words—it’s about creating something that feels human and trustworthy. In a world that’s moving towards automation, the human touch sets skilled freelance writers apart. That’s why I believe we’re seeing this resurgence—it’s not a trend; it’s a return to what really matters in writing.

FAQ Section

Why are clients choosing human writers over AI-generated content?
Because human writers bring a level of connection and depth that AI can’t replicate. It’s about making sure the reader feels understood.

What’s the difference between SEO and AEO?
SEO focuses on getting content to rank high in search results. AEO is about optimizing content for answer engines and voice search, providing clear, direct answers to user queries.

How does EEAT impact content quality?
EEAT (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness) is all about building credibility. It ensures that readers can trust the content and the person behind it.

Can freelancers use AI without losing their personal voice?
Absolutely. It’s about using AI as a tool, not a crutch. The key is to maintain control over the final voice and style.

What’s the best way for new writers to build a strong reputation?
Consistency, honesty, and a focus on quality. Show that you can adapt and bring something valuable to the table.

AI Content Writing & Skilled Writers Unite

We live in a time where AI can draft essays, write articles, and suggest ways to optimize content for search engines. It’s impressive, sure—but there’s something AI can’t replace: the human connection. That’s where content writers come in, bringing creativity and insight to create stories that readers truly care about.

AI can analyze numbers, spot trends, and even create a rough draft. But when it comes to understanding the heart of a story or making a message feel relatable, only a writer can do the job. Writers turn AI’s data into content that people remember. It’s not just about informing readers—it’s about leaving an impact.

For example, AI might propose writing about trending social media topics, but it takes a writer to add the emotional depth and nuance that makes the piece stand out. By working together, AI and content writers create something better than either could on their own.

How AI Fits into Content Creation

AI tools can simplify many parts of the writing process. Tasks like checking grammar, analyzing keywords, and brainstorming content ideas can now be done in minutes. This saves time and allows writers to focus on what matters most—crafting engaging and authentic content.

Data analysis is another area where AI excels. It can highlight what audiences are searching for, how they engage with certain topics, and what trends are worth exploring. Writers can use this information to create targeted content that answers real questions and provides value.

AI can also support writers with first drafts. For example, tools that generate rough outlines or initial ideas can help writers overcome blank-page syndrome. These drafts aren’t perfect but serve as a starting point that a human writer can refine into something meaningful.

Why Writers Are Still Essential

No matter how advanced AI becomes, it can’t replace the creativity and emotion of human writers. A great article isn’t just about presenting facts; it’s about making readers feel understood. Writers know how to connect with people by using language that feels personal and real.

Context is another thing AI struggles with. While it might understand data, it often misses cultural nuances, humor, and subtleties of tone. Writers can adapt their style to suit different audiences and situations, ensuring the message lands in the right way.

Originality is another reason writers remain vital. AI relies on patterns and existing data, which can lead to repetitive or generic ideas. Writers, on the other hand, can create fresh content by drawing from their experiences, observations, and imagination.

How AI and Writers Work Together

The best results come when AI and writers collaborate. AI takes care of repetitive or technical tasks, giving writers more time to focus on creative work. This partnership allows businesses to produce content faster without sacrificing quality.

For example, AI can suggest which keywords to use for better visibility or analyze how well previous content has performed. Writers can use this data to refine their work, making it more impactful. Together, they achieve a balance of efficiency and creativity that benefits both businesses and readers.

Personalization Through AI

AI’s ability to analyze user behavior makes personalization easier. It can help segment audiences based on their interests, habits, or demographics. With this information, writers can tailor their content to specific groups, creating a stronger connection that keeps readers engaged.

Tailored content isn’t just about the message but also the tone, style, and timing. AI tools can suggest the best time to share content for the best results. Writers use these insights to deliver material that feels relevant and timely, improving the overall experience for readers.

Overcoming Challenges with AI in Content Creation

While AI is a valuable tool, it comes with its challenges. AI-generated content can sometimes feel cold or robotic. This is why the writer’s role in adding personality and depth is so important. A well-told story or a unique perspective can turn ordinary content into something unforgettable.

There’s also the risk of relying too much on AI. While data is helpful, overanalyzing trends can make content feel forced or formulaic. Writers must strike a balance between following data and trusting their instincts to create content that truly stands out.

Finally, technology changes quickly, and staying updated can be overwhelming. Writers who embrace learning and adaptability will continue to thrive, using AI as a tool to enhance their skills rather than replace them.

Looking Ahead: AI and the Future of Writing

As AI continues to improve, it’s likely to play an even bigger role in content creation. Tools may become smarter, offering deeper insights into audience preferences or providing more creative suggestions. This will allow writers to push boundaries, creating meaningful stories while using AI to handle the technical side of things.

Emerging technologies like augmented reality and virtual reality will also create new opportunities for content creators. Imagine creating interactive experiences that allow readers to engage with stories in entirely new ways. While these technological advancements are exciting, one thing remains certain: the human touch will always be essential in creating content that matters.

AI and content writers aren’t competitors—they’re partners. Together, they bring the best of both worlds: efficiency and creativity, data and emotion. Businesses that embrace this partnership will find themselves better equipped to connect with their audiences in ways that feel authentic and impactful.

Ready to take your content strategy to the next level? Let’s explore how we can work together. Email me at inquiry@ginoborlado.org, and let’s start creating content that truly connects.

Mornings are for writing. It’s when my head’s clear enough to put thoughts into words before the noise of the day sets in.

But lately, even before I start drafting, my Facebook feed looks like a landfill of recycled propaganda, recycled lines, the same DDS pages that never seem to die.

It’s strange because I had to start over. The original Morning Coffee Thoughts page got hacked, so I built a new one from scratch. Now the platform doesn’t seem to recognize me.

Since I often write about the garbage the Dutertes left behind, the system seems to think I’m one of their fans. It’s feeding me their content like it’s doing me a favor.

I’ve known for a while that there’s an unseen filter shaping what reaches us online. But this is the first time it’s felt personal. This blog takes a serious look at how our feeds are shaped—how they track, guess, and decide what we see—and what we can do to stop the system from promoting propaganda. A guide to taking back control, cleaning up your Facebook feed, and blocking the DDS.

Because every scroll, every pause, every click teaches it something about who we are. And the more I look at what it’s showing me, the more I realize: it’s not just the content that shapes people. The system shaping the content needs watching too.

## Understanding How Social Media Builds Your Feed

The feed you see isn’t random. It’s built piece by piece by a system that studies what catches your eye. Every scroll, every pause, every reaction feeds into that invisible process that decides what shows up next.

It begins with what’s called inventory — all the posts available to you. That includes updates from friends, pages you follow, groups you’ve joined, and even public posts you’ve never interacted with before ([SocialBee](https://socialbee.com/blog/facebook-algorithm/), [Hootsuite](https://blog.hootsuite.com/facebook-algorithm/)).

Then comes the signals phase. The platform looks at hundreds of data points: who posted it, what kind of content it is, when it was shared, and how you’ve interacted with similar posts in the past ([Planable](https://planable.io/blog/guide-to-social-media-algorithms/)).

After that, the system makes predictions. It tries to guess how you’ll behave — whether you’ll like, share, comment, or watch a video till the end. Each prediction helps it learn what to prioritize.

Finally, everything gets a relevance score. The higher the score, the more likely it appears at the top of your feed ([Buffer](https://buffer.com/resources/facebook-algorithm/)).

The cycle repeats every time you scroll. It’s fast, constant, and fine-tuned to keep you from leaving. Some argue these systems are neutral, but neutrality in this context is tricky. When engagement becomes the measure of value, whatever gets the most reactions ends up dominating the feed ([Knight Columbia](https://knightcolumbia.org/content/understanding-social-media-recommendation-algorithms)).

That’s why the same posts, faces, and political pages keep resurfacing, even when you’ve moved on. The system isn’t trying to understand you — it’s trying to keep you scrolling.

## How the System Tracks Your Behavior

The moment you open an app, it starts watching. Not in a paranoid way — just quietly, constantly, and very, very fast. Everything you do tells it something.

It begins with what you click, read, and watch. Each reaction — like, comment, share, or even just a few seconds of viewing — becomes a clue to your interests ([Tencent Cloud](https://www.tencentcloud.com/techpedia/119237), [Wired](https://www.wired.com/story/tiktok-data-privacy/)). On TikTok, for example, your viewing time, likes, follows, and even the kind of videos you linger on form a detailed behavioral profile ([Sage Journals](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/20563051221086241)).

Then comes dwell time — how long your eyes stay on a post before you move on. It’s one of the strongest signals that the system measures. Even without liking or commenting, just staring at something for a few seconds tells it to find more of the same ([The Social Content Factory](https://thesocialcontentfactory.com/blog/major-update-instagrams-2025-algorithm-prioritizes-dwell-time), [Prominence Global](https://www.prominence.global/linkedins-dwell-time-how-to-create-content-that-keeps-users-engaged), [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.10464)).

It also pays attention to how you scroll. Every pause, flick, and hesitation becomes a datapoint. Slowing down on a certain post tells it to find more of the same. Speeding past something tells it to show less of that in the future ([Amplitude](https://amplitude.com/blog/3-metrics-modeling-mobile-user-behavior), [Rose & Gold](https://roseandgold.com/the-science-of-scrolling-understanding-user-behavior-on-social-platforms)).

The scariest part is that you don’t even have to engage for it to learn from you. Just looking counts. It’s a strange feeling — knowing your silence online is still being studied.

## The Technical Side: What Your Smartphone Sensors Reveal

Phones today do more than track what you click. They can also sense how you look, move, and react. This isn’t science fiction; it’s already part of how most modern devices work.

Some apps can estimate where your eyes are focused using just the front camera. Studies have shown that with the right hardware, your phone can follow your eye movement with surprising accuracy ([Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-18360-5), [PubMed Central](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8975177/)).

Then there’s facial expression analysis — systems that detect micro-expressions to guess what you’re feeling while scrolling. It’s not just theoretical anymore; researchers have mapped emotional responses in real time using facial data captured through the camera ([JMIR](https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e68942), [UniBW](https://www.unibw.de/usable-security-and-privacy/publikationen/pdf/kosch2020avi.pdf)).

Every tap and swipe also leaves a trace. Touch behavior — the way you scroll, the rhythm of your typing, the pressure of your press — can reveal patterns unique to you ([PubMed Central](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6686626/), [Kostakos.org](http://kostakos.org/)).

And behind all that, your phone is quietly counting how long you spend on each screen. Some apps even track how often you tilt the page to calculate how long your attention stays in one place ([Medical Xpress](https://medicalxpress.com/news/2025-08-hour-day-social-media-scrolling.html)).

It’s easy to forget how much these small movements add up. Every second, your phone is building a picture of how you think, react, and linger. Not through what you say — but through what your body unconsciously gives away.

## The “Always Listening” Microphone: Eavesdropping and Ad Targeting

Phones today don’t just watch. They listen too — even when you’re not talking to them.

Voice assistants like “Hey Siri” or “OK Google” keep the microphone active so they can respond anytime. But it doesn’t stop when other apps also have microphone access. If you’ve granted permission, they can pick up snippets of your conversation even when the app isn’t open ([Norton](https://us.norton.com/blog/how-to/is-my-phone-listening-to-me), [Surfshark](https://surfshark.com/blog/is-my-phone-listening-to-me), [Spiralytics](https://www.spiralytics.com/blog/mobile-ads-can-phone-hear-conversations-infographic/)).

Ever notice how you mention a product in conversation, then suddenly see ads for it? That isn’t coincidence. Audio segments can be analyzed for keywords and linked to your profile for ad targeting. The recordings are often deleted, but the extracted data remains ([New Atlas](https://newatlas.com/computers/smartphone-listening-conversations-ads-facebook/)).

The fix is simple, though not automatic. Check which apps have microphone access. Deny the ones that don’t need it to function. Most people forget these settings exist — and that’s exactly why background listening goes unnoticed.

Once I reviewed mine, I realized dozens of apps I never use still had permission to hear me. It was a good time to clear them out and finally switch them off.

Our devices already collect so much. We can’t stop every form of tracking, but we can draw a line with the things that feel like they’re crossing it. What we say in the room through the mic should stay private.

## Platform Policies, Moderation, and Legal Remedies

There’s another layer to all this — one that decides which voices get amplified and which quietly fade away.

When a post or page is reported as spam or misinformation by enough people, Facebook and similar platforms often lower its visibility. It can be flagged, hidden from timelines, or placed under review without the user ever knowing why. These user reports, whether coordinated or not, often dictate what the public gets to see ([Internet Governance Forum](https://www.intgovforum.org/sites/default/files/webform/ssrn-id3792097.pdf)).

The process sounds fair on paper, but it isn’t always. Some groups use reporting tools to silence criticism or debate. I’ve seen people lose their pages overnight — not because they spread lies, but because their truths upset the wrong audience.

Still, there are real protections in place for those who experience digital harassment or defamation. In the Philippines, online abuse and organized misinformation campaigns can fall under cybercrime and privacy laws. Victims can report to the NBI or consult experts who specialize in online accountability ([Respicio.ph](http://respicio.ph/)).

Social media often feels lawless, but that’s not entirely true. The same tools used to manipulate your feed can also be used to defend it — if you know your rights and how to respond.

## Step-by-Step: Blocking and Filtering on Major Platforms

After learning how these systems read us, I started cleaning up my own feed. It’s not dramatic work, but these are small steps that make your space a little lighter each time.

### Facebook
*   Go to any post and tap the three dots. Choose **Hide post** or **Snooze [account]**.
*   To stop seeing a page altogether, open it and hit **Block**.
*   You can also fine-tune your feed under **Settings > News Feed Preferences**. From there, unfollow, snooze, or prioritize who you actually want to hear from. ([Facebook Help Center](https://www.facebook.com/help) via [SocialBee](https://socialbee.com/blog/facebook-algorithm/))

### Instagram
*   Tap the three dots on a post and select **Not Interested** or **Report**.
*   For persistent accounts, go to their profile, tap the three dots again, and hit **Block**. ([Instagram Support](https://help.instagram.com) via [Hootsuite](https://blog.hootsuite.com/facebook-algorithm/))

### TikTok
*   Long-press any video and choose **Not Interested** or **Report**.
*   If a user keeps showing up on your “For You” page, open their profile, tap the three dots, and select **Block**. ([TikTok Safety Center](https://www.tiktok.com/safety) via [Wired](https://www.wired.com/story/tiktok-data-privacy/))

Each platform updates these settings often, so it helps to check their help pages every few months. Small actions add up. Blocking one account doesn’t feel like much, but over time your feed begins to look like yours again.

## The Tolerance Trap: Why Scrolling Past Content Backfires

There was a time when I thought ignoring DDS content was enough. Scroll fast, don’t engage, and maybe it’ll stop showing up. I was wrong.

Every time you pause on a post, even for a heartbeat, the system interprets it as interest. It doesn’t care if you’re angry or amused—it only measures time and attention. That’s how hate pages keep slipping back into your feed.

Dwell time, scroll speed, and passive viewing all feed the same machine that curates your timeline. The more you watch, the stronger the signal that you “like” that kind of content ([Metricool](https://metricool.com/social-media-algorithms/), [Dash Social](https://www.dashsocial.com/blog/facebook-algorithm), [Brandwatch](https://www.brandwatch.com/blog/social-media-algorithm/)).

Blocking, muting, or marking posts as “Not Interested” sends the opposite signal. It teaches the system what you don’t want to see. It’s the difference between ignoring the noise and actually building a wall against it.

Arguing in the comments does the worst kind of damage—it tells the platform the post is engaging, making it more visible to others.

Silence, when it means scrolling past, isn’t neutrality. It’s fuel. And that’s how the algorithm keeps the garbage floating to the top.

## The Blocking Strategy: Taking Active Control

After learning how the system reacts to every move we make, the next step is simple but deliberate: active filtering.

*   Block, hide, or report any account or page that spreads propaganda or disinformation.
*   Unfollow and mute contacts whose posts drain your focus.
*   Use keyword filters when the option exists.
*   And most importantly, never comment, argue, or react to bait posts—each response tells the system to keep that content on your feed ([SocialBu](https://socialbu.com/blog/social-media-content-filtering)).

If you’re part of an online group or advocacy page, take it further. Encourage **mass blocking** and **mass reporting** when coordinated disinformation campaigns appear. These actions push the system to lower the visibility of those pages faster.

Reclaiming control isn’t a one-time act. It’s a habit. The more consistently you block and filter, the cleaner your timeline becomes. You’re not just cleaning your feed; you’re deciding what kind of conversation deserves to be seen.

## The Limits of User Control

Even with all the blocking, filtering, and cleaning up, there’s only so much we can control. These systems are built to keep us engaged, not to protect our peace.

DDS content can easily reappear under new names or recycled pages. Sometimes, even when a post gets reported, it stays through because it doesn’t technically break any platform rule. And then there are sponsored posts—ads that will find you whatever you tell the feed you don’t want to see ([SocialBu](https://socialbu.com/blog/social-media-content-filtering)).

Browser tools can help a little. Extensions that filter keywords or mute pages can reduce noise, though they don’t stop the tracking completely ([TechLockdown](https://www.techlockdown.com/guides/block-social-media)).

There’s no such thing as a perfectly clean timeline. Platforms change constantly, and what works today might be different tomorrow. But cleaning up isn’t just a technical act — it’s a form of self-defense.

When you start filtering what you let in, you’re not escaping reality. You’re choosing which parts of it you want to focus on.

## Privacy, Ethics, and Mindful Tech Use

At some point, it stops feeling like technology and starts feeling like intrusion. Every scroll, tap, or glance becomes another entry in a data file you’ll never see.

The truth is, constant tracking and background sensors raise real questions about consent. Many of us clicked “Accept” without reading the fine print, and now those quiet permissions have turned into a permanent spotlight. These data-hungry systems trade in what we read, what we buy, and how long we stare at a screen ([FS Poster](https://www.fs-poster.com/blog/how-social-media-sites-use-your-personal-data)).

Privacy laws exist, but enforcement is patchy. What’s legal in one country might be abuse in another. It’s why we have to review app permissions, and use browser tools that reduce tracking. The goal isn’t paranoia—it’s awareness.

There’s also the mental side of all this. Doom-scrolling through noise and hostility takes a toll. Studies consistently link heavy exposure to polarizing content with higher stress and anxiety ([PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11804976/), [Information Matters](https://informationmatters.org/2025/02/the-double-edged-scroll-active-vs-passive-social-media-use-and-stress/)). The mind wasn’t built to handle endless outrage.

Learning to scroll with intention matters. Curate your feed like you curate your surroundings. You are the only one who can decide what deserves your time and attention. The line between awareness and exhaustion is thin—don’t let an algorithm start mistaking noise for truth.

## Collective, National Action and Advocacy

Cleaning up your own feed helps, but the fight doesn’t end there. What we see online isn’t shaped by one person alone — it’s the product of millions of clicks feeding a machine.

When coordinated misinformation drives flood timelines, individual blocking isn’t enough. That’s where collective action matters. Educate friends, family, and coworkers about how engagement works. Encourage them to report troll pages and propaganda networks when they appear. When whole communities move in sync, the system starts to shift.

Schools can also play a part. Digital literacy should be as basic as reading and writing — teaching young Filipinos how to question what they see, check sources, and recognize manipulation. It’s not paranoia; it’s survival in an environment designed to capture attention, not tell the truth.

At a larger scale, we need stronger policies. Lawmakers and regulators should demand transparency about how these feeds are built and moderated. Platforms must be held accountable for how their systems shape public opinion and political discourse ([Knight Columbia](https://knightcolumbia.org/content/understanding-social-media-recommendation-algorithms)).

It’s time to treat digital misinformation the same way we treat pollution — something that doesn’t just exist online but spills over into real life, poisoning how people think, vote, and decide.

## Empower Yourself and Your Community

Awareness only matters if it turns into action. Each block, unfollow, or report teaches the system what kind of content doesn’t belong in your space.

Show others how to do it. Not through lectures or long debates, but through quiet example. When people see your feed free of trolls and noise, they’ll start asking how you did it — and that’s where the real teaching begins.

Be mindful of what you engage with. Every second you spend on a post, even in silence, tells the system to show you more of it. Guard your attention like something valuable. Protect it from the things designed to make you angry, tired, or numb.

We can’t rely on platforms to clean up the mess. Change begins when users stop rewarding the noise with attention.

So the next time a DDS post appears on your screen, don’t argue, don’t react, and don’t stay. Block it. Report it. Move on.

When enough people do this, the system learns a new pattern — one that favors awareness over outrage.

The internet reflects what we feed it. Let it mirror a country that values truth.

And yes, block the DDS.
